coredata.dc:title: Sustainable energy harvesting: Manganese oxide-decorated carbon quantum dots derived from agriculture for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Al Kiey, Sherief A.
Tohamy, Hebat-Allah S.
coredata.dc:description: As the global demand for efficient energy storage devices intensifies, a growing imperative exists to develop sustainable and high-performance materials for supercapacitors. This study presents an approach to address this challenge by synthesizing manganese oxide (Mn2O3)-decorated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from agricultural waste. The integration of Mn2O3 with carbon quantum dots extracted from readily available agricultural residue yields a unique hybrid material exhibiting exceptional properties tailored for high-performance supercapacitors. Several techniques were employed to fully characterize the hybrid material. These included XRD, XPS, surface area analysis, IR, TEM, and SEM-EDX. The electrochemical performance of the synthesized composite was comprehensively investigated through cyclic voltammetry, charge-discharge studies, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The synthesized Mn2O3/CQD2 electrodes achieved a high specific capacitance of 612 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in a three-electrode arrangement. CV studies revealed that the Mn2O3/CQD2 electrode exhibited a greater specific capacitance of 657.1 F g−1 at 5 mV/s. After 5000 cycles, the Mn2O3/CQD2 electrode exhibited a retention rate of 96.3 % and a coulombic efficiency of 98 %, indicating exceptional performance. This research not only advances the understanding of hybrid materials but also propels us toward a more sustainable and circular approach to energy storage technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Agricultural waste
Manganese oxide
Supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behavior of the as-prepared Mn2O3/CQD electrode

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) XPS survey profiles of the Mn2O3/CQD electrodes, b) High resolution spectra of Mn, c, d) High resolution spectra of O and C of as-synthesized materials.
XRD spectra of the as-synthesized Mn2O3/CQD composites.
a, b) Low and high magnification TEM, c, d) low and high magnification TEM, and e–f) low and high magnification SEM images with EDX analysis of Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2.
N2 physisorption isotherms, and b) pore size distribution of the Mn2O3/CQD composites.
FTIR spectra of (a) Mn2O3/CQD 1 and (b) Mn2O3/CQD 2 composites.
Thermal analysis of (a) Mn2O3/CQD 1 and (b) Mn2O3/CQD 2.
Contact angle measurements for a) Mn2O3/CQD 1 and b) Mn2O3/CQD 2.
Comparison of the CV curves of the Mn2O3/CQD composites at 100 mV/s.
CV patterns of the a) Mn2O3/CQD1 and b) Mn2O3/CQD2 electrodes at different scan rates and c) relation between the specific capacitance and scan rate in 1 M Na2SO4.
Contribution from the surface and diffusion-controlled processes for Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrode.
GCD profiles of the a) Mn2O3/CQD 1 and b) Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrodes at different current densities and c) relation between the specific capacitance and current density in 1 M Na2SO4.
Stability of Mn2O3/CQD composites at 10 A/g for 5000 cycles.
a) Nyquist plot and b) phase angle of the as-synthesized electrodes.
Comparison of our synthesized electrodes with those reported in the literature.

head.ce:title: Sustainable energy harvesting: Manganese oxide-decorated carbon quantum dots derived from agriculture for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry and Corrosion Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, National Research Centre, Dokki, Cairo 12622, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrochemistry and Corrosion Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, National Research Centre, Dokki, Cairo 12622, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material Engineering Lab, Central Laboratories Network, National Research Centre, Dokki, Cairo 12622, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Material Engineering Lab, Central Laboratories Network, National Research Centre, Dokki, Cairo 12622, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, Dokki, 12622 Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cellulose and Paper Department, National Research Centre, Dokki, 12622, Giza, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Electrochemistry and Corrosion Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, National Research Centre, Dokki, Cairo 12622, Egypt.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the global demand for efficient energy storage devices intensifies, a growing imperative exists to develop sustainable and high-performance materials for supercapacitors. This study presents an approach to address this challenge by synthesizing manganese oxide (Mn2O3)-decorated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from agricultural waste. The integration of Mn2O3 with carbon quantum dots extracted from readily available agricultural residue yields a unique hybrid material exhibiting exceptional properties tailored for high-performance supercapacitors. Several techniques were employed to fully characterize the hybrid material. These included XRD, XPS, surface area analysis, IR, TEM, and SEM-EDX. The electrochemical performance of the synthesized composite was comprehensively investigated through cyclic voltammetry, charge-discharge studies, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The synthesized Mn2O3/CQD2 electrodes achieved a high specific capacitance of 612 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 in a three-electrode arrangement. CV studies revealed that the Mn2O3/CQD2 electrode exhibited a greater specific capacitance of 657.1 F g−1 at 5 mV/s. After 5000 cycles, the Mn2O3/CQD2 electrode exhibited a retention rate of 96.3 % and a coulombic efficiency of 98 %, indicating exceptional performance. This research not only advances the understanding of hybrid materials but also propels us toward a more sustainable and circular approach to energy storage technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Global energy issues are now recognized as significant obstacles to progress in society and the economy, representing a potential threat to the survival of humanity. Compared with traditional capacitors, supercapacitors (SCs) have demonstrated outstanding performance as energy storage devices because of their exceptional specific power. Furthermore, they provide cost-effective and environmentally favorable characteristics [1,2]. Supercapacitor devices have a comparatively low energy density, which has rendered their widespread utilization challenging. Currently, various research efforts are focusing on the rational design of outstanding novel materials for constructing exceptional electrodes with high energy density. Nonetheless, the electrochemical characteristics of supercapacitors are significantly influenced by the chemical composition and the geometry of the electrode materials. Consequently, improving the performance of supercapacitors by further developing their beneficial characteristics is a highly attractive area of research. Extensive research has been carried out on various materials with the objective of boosting supercapacitor device performance. These materials include conductive polymers, metal sulfides, graphene, carbon nanotubes, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), doped porous carbon, metal hydroxides, and metal oxides.

Metal oxides have been extensively investigated for a variety of active materials for SCs due to the redox mechanisms provided to achieve specific capacitances [3–8]. However, the efficiency of supercapacitors is hindered by their low rate, insufficient conductivity, and low stability [9,10]. Numerous researchers have combined metal oxides with conductive carbon-based fibers to circumvent the aforementioned drawbacks.

Pseudocapacitive materials comprising manganese oxides under various oxidation states represent one of the most promising, attractive, and frequently utilized alternatives because of their wide potential window, substantial specific capacitance, affordability, and negligible environmental impact [11,12]. However, they have insufficient electronic conductivity, leading the particles to leach out while cycling, which causes a low power density as well as limited cycle life [13]. Several academics have attempted to integrate manganese-based materials into a highly conducting matrix, such as carbon, to increase their efficiency. Wang et al. constructed and developed uniform MnO particles on conductive carbon cloth to attain specific capacitance values of F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 [14]. Zhu et al. implemented a two-step chemical procedure to produce MnO2 nanoflakes, which delivered a specific capacitance of 178 F g−1 at 5 mV/s [15]. According to Du et al. [16], the MnOx-CNF mat reached 211 F g−1 at 0.25 A g−1 in a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a unique class of nanomaterials composed of carbon-based quasispherical particles that are frequently less than 10 nm in diameter. They are conjugated sp2/sp3 cores containing various functional groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, and aldehyde groups [17,18]. Carbon quantum dots are extremely tiny carbon particles that possess several beneficial properties, including uniform dispersion, superior electron transfer/reservoir ability photoluminescent features, and enhanced edge quantum effects compared to those of other carbon materials [17,19,20]. In addition to conserving the superior optical characteristics of typical semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs compensate for the weaknesses of these materials with regard to their cytotoxicity, environmental impact, and biohazard. Moreover, CQDs have excellent water solubility, chemical stability, surface functionalization ease, and large-scale preparation capabilities [21]. CQDs demonstrate significant potential as multifunctional nanoparticles for various purposes, such as chemical sensing, biosensing, gas sensing, photodynamic treatment, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, and energy storage [22–25]. Carbon quantum dots have been produced through a wide range of techniques. These include hydrothermal treatment, laser ablation of carbon base materials, microwave-assisted synthesis, soot purification from arc discharge studies, pyrolysis of glycerol, electrochemical oxidation of graphite rods, plasma treatment of single-layer graphene thermal carbonization of molecules, acidic treatment of carbon fibers, thermal annealing of barbecue char, and atmospheric plasma-based synthesis [23,26–29]. Activated carbons derived from lignin were uniformly coated with CDs according to Harper et al. to produce composite materials. The CD@AC-11 electrode exhibits an outstanding capacitance of 301.7 F g−1 when maintained at a current density of 0.15 A g−1 [30]. The C60 powder was used as a precursor by Zhu et al. to generate CDs. At a current density of 5 A g−1, it is possible to attain a specific capacitance of 106 F g−1 [31]. To achieve better specific capacity, desirable rate capability, and potential complementary range, Wu et al. constructed 3D porous hierarchical carbon quantum dots (CQDs) composites [32]. Tailored CQDs composites for ACs were produced by Hou et al. The composite electrode of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nickel cobalt oxide (NiCo2O4) has a significant specific capacitance of 856 F g−1 at an applied current density of 1 A g−1 [33]. Supercapacitor electrodes with a specific capacitance of 199 F g−1 at 0.5 A/g have been prepared using CQDs produced from banana skins through a one-step hydrothermal process [34]. It was also recently revealed that visible light may increase the specific capacitance of carbon dots-based supercapacitors. In the presence of visible light, a specific capacitance of 312 F g−1 was attained at 0.1 A/g, which was more than twice the value obtained when there was no light present [35]. Vanalakar have successfully employed a simple hydrothermal approach to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using turmeric leaves, resulting in a green and cost-effective process. The electrode material constructed from CQDs demonstrates remarkable electrocapacitive performance, achieving a maximum specific capacitance of 467.87 F g−1 [36]. The unique physicochemical properties of carbon quantum dots, coupled with their sustainable sourcing from agricultural remnants, make them environmentally friendly alternatives to conventional electrode materials. Conversely, the electrochemical characteristics of CDs can be significantly modified and improved through the doping of additional heteroatoms (e.g., N, P, S, and O) and the manipulation of diverse surface functional groups. This highlights the critical nature of CDs in the field of energy storage [37,38].

Therefore, the synthesis of Mn2O3-decorated carbon quantum dots from agricultural waste represents a compelling pathway toward environmentally friendly and high-performance supercapacitors. The multi-technique characterizations and thorough electrochemical analysis provide comprehensive insight into the material's structure, performance, and stability, contributing to the advancement of sustainable energy storage technologies.

Sugarcane bagasse was obtained from the Paper Industry Quena Company, Egypt, and used to prepare cellulose, which was modified to prepare carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) according to our previous work [39]. Mn2O3/CQD were synthesized by mixing 1.8 g of potassium permanganate with 0.3 g of CMC in 100 ml of H2O. Then, 0.9 g of manganese acetate was added to the previous mixture to prepare Mn2O3/CQD 1, and 1.8 g of manganese acetate was added to the previous mixture to prepare Mn2O3/CQD 2. The two separate mixtures were homogenized, transferred to a stainless steel autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. After that, the Mn2O3/CQD composites were collected, washed, and dried. The as-prepared materials were calcined at 500 °C for 2 h.

The structure and morphology were examined using transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM-2100F) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, QUANTA FEG 250.). The composition of the as-synthesized materials was studied using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Jasco PS-4000) was employed to acquire infrared spectra. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was applied to verify the valence states as well as the chemical composition of the elements embedded in the samples (XPS, Thermo). The specific surface areas of the prepared composites were evaluated at 77 K using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) approach. The thermal stability of the material was examined using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, TG Themsys One+, SETARAM, France) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in an atmosphere of N2.

To evaluate the electrochemical characteristics of the synthesized samples, three-electrode and two-electrode symmetric systems were assembled. A three-electrode arrangement was used for all electrochemical investigations employing platinum wire as the counter electrode, while a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was employed as the reference electrode. The working electrode was constructed from Ni foam coated with a mixture of 10 wt% PVDF, 10 wt% carbon black, and 80 wt% active materials in NMP. Na2SO4 (1 M) was utilized as the electrolyte. To ensure full drying, the electrodes were oven-dried for 24 h after deposition at 60 °C. Voltalab 40 (France) potentiostat was used for all the electrochemical studies. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis was carried out on the electrodes throughout the potential range of 0–0.6 V at different speed rates in the range from 5 to 100 mV/s. The GCD measurements, on the other hand, were carried out in the potential range of 0–0.45 V at current densities ranging from 1 to 10 A/g. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) study used an open circuit potential and measured frequencies between 10 mHz and 100,000 Hz.

The specific capacitances (Cs, F g−1) of the working electrodes were calculated using the following equations [40]:
(1)
C s
=
1
mv ∆ V
∫
V 0
V
I
V
dV
For
CV
(2)
C s
= I ∆ t / S ∆ V
For
GCD
where m is the mass of the electrode in g, v is the scan rate in mV s−1, ΔV is the potential window in V, and
∫
V 0
V
I
V
dV represents the integral area of the CV curve, where I denotes the current.

Surface analysis using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed to evaluate the surface characteristics, chemical composition, and chemical state of the elements on the surfaces of Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 . The survey scan revealed the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and manganese (Mn) peaks, confirming the successful incorporation of Mn2O3 into the carbon quantum dots. The presence of Mn peaks alongside C and O further validates the presence of the decorated structure. The high-resolution scan of C 1 s provided insights into the carbon functional groups present in the material. The C 1 s high-resolution scan showed distinct peaks corresponding to C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.5 eV), and COO (288.5 eV), providing insights into the carbon-based functional groups present in the material (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c displays the O 1 s XPS spectra. The deconvolution analysis of the O1s peak of Mn2O3/CQD 1 revealed the existence of three distinct peaks at binding energies of 530.40 eV, 531.6 eV, and 533 eV. These peaks can be attributed to the presence of H-O-C=O, CO, and -C-OH, respectively. The two peaks located at 653.6 eV and 641.8 eV in Fig. 1d can be attributed to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2, respectively. The energy difference between these two peaks is 11.8 eV, which is comparable to the spin-orbit separation of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2. This suggests that Mn2O3 formed on the surface of the composite [41].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is a powerful technique used to analyze the crystal structure of materials. The XRD patterns of the Mn2O3/CQD composites are presented in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern reveals distinct peaks, allowing for the identification of crystalline phases present in the Mn2O3/CQD composite. The major peaks at 2θ values of 28.5°, 36.2°, 56.7°, 45°, and 52° correspond well with the crystallographic planes (104), (110), (113), (202), and (116) of rhombohedral Mn2O3 (JCPDS Card No. 44–1472). This suggests the predominant presence of Mn2O3 in the composite. Given the amorphous or disordered nature of carbon quantum dots, their contribution to the XRD pattern is less discernible. No distinct peaks related to carbon phases are observed, which is consistent with the expected XRD behavior of amorphous carbon materials. The sharp and well-defined peaks in the XRD pattern indicate the crystalline nature of Mn2O3 in the composite. The absence of impurity peaks suggests a high degree of phase purity in the synthesized Mn2O3/CQD composites. The broadening of the XRD peaks was analyzed using the Scherrer equation to estimate the crystallite size. The average crystallite sizes of the Mn2O3/CQD1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 composites were calculated to be approximately 1.9 and 2.6 nm, respectively, which are indicative of nanoscale dimensions [42].

TEM analysis of Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 revealed that the pure CQDs were spherical in shape as shown in Fig. 3a–d. The particle size of Mn2O3/CQD 1 (i.e., 1.78 nm) is smaller than that of Mn2O3/CQD 2 (i.e., 2.42 nm) which is in agreement with XRD data. This may be due to the high content of Mn on Mn2O3/CQD 2, which accumulates and increases the particle size. No obvious CQDs appeared in the SEM images. This may be due to the crumbling of the samples during storage. The Mn values calculated from EDX were 14.73 and 50.41 % for Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD, respectively.

The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the Mn2O3/CQDs was determined using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method, as illustrated in Fig. 4 a and b. BET analysis revealed specific surface areas of 209 and 451 m2/g for Mn2O3/CQD1 and Mn2O3/CQD2, respectively. This result indicates a substantial surface area, suggesting high degree of exposed active sites on the nanomaterial. The observed surface area is intricately linked to the pore structure of the material. The BET analysis not only revealed the specific surface area but also revealed a diverse range of pores, including mesopores and micropores. This pore architecture facilitates efficient ion diffusion and accessibility, which are crucial factors for rapid charge and discharge cycles in supercapacitor applications. The pore size distribution is crucial for determining the suitability of the nanocomposite for supercapacitor applications crucial for maximizing charge storage capacity and ensuring efficient ion transport. The average pore diameter of the Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 nanocomposites was determined to be 10 and 13.4 nm, respectively. The Mn2O3/CQD nanocomposites indicate uniformity in pore size distribution, which can enhance performance in applications requiring consistent pore sizes. The correlation between the surface area and supercapacitor performance is evident. A higher surface area correlates with increased capacitance, faster charge transfer, and improved cycling stability. Mn2O3/CQDs, with their augmented surface area, hold promise for high-performance supercapacitor applications.

The IR spectra of the prepared Mn2O3/CQD1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 show absorption bands between 3270 and 3332, 1652–1641, 1546–1548, 1405–1450 and 1342–1388 cm−1 attributed to OH, CO, CC, C–O=C and C–O–C stretching vibrations as depicted in Fig. 5 . In addition, the peak between 489 and 522 cm−1 was attributed to MnO [43,44]. The OH groups exhibited a relative absorbance (RA) of 0.56 and 1.20 in Mn2O3/CQD1 and Mn2O3/CQD2, respectively, which indicates that Mn2O3/CQD 2 is more hydrophilic than Mn2O3/CQD1. In addition, the RAs of the MnO peak were 0.84 and 1.26 for Mn2O3/CQD1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2, respectively. This may be due to the high concentration of Mn on Mn2O3/CQD1.

The activation energy (Ea) from the thermal decomposition was calculated from the thermal analysis data. Using the Coats-Redfern method, Eqs. (3) and (4) were utilized.
(3)
log
1 −
1 − a
1 − n
T 2
1 − n
= log
AR βE
1 −
2 RT
E
−
E
2.303 RT
for
n ≠ 1
(4)
log
− log
1 − a
T 2
= log
AR βE
1 −
2 RT
E
−
E
2.303 RT
for
n = 1
in which n denotes the degradation reaction order, α denotes the fractional conversion, β denotes the heating rate in K/min, T denotes the temperature in Kelvin, and R denotes the gas constant in kJ mol−1. K), A denotes the frequency factor in (s−1), and E denotes the activation energy. A straight-line relationship should be noted when plotting the relationship between variables using various suitable values of n, as stated by Eqs. (3) and (4). For the purpose of calculating the standard error (SE) and calculating the correlation coefficient (r) for each value of n (ranging from 0 to 3.0), the approach known as least squares was implemented utilizing various n values. From the slope, the activation energy was estimated (E/2.303R), whereas the frequency factor A was calculated from the intercept (log AR/ßE) of the Coats-Redfern equation by the most appropriate value of n. The other kinetic parameters, including enthalpy ( ∆ H), entropy ( ∆ S) and free energy change ( ∆ G), were estimated employing Eq. (5).
(5)
ΔH ∗
=
E ∗
− RT ;
ΔG ∗
=
ΔH ∗
−
TΔS ∗
and
ΔS ∗
= 2.303
log
Ah KT
R
where (h) and (k) are the Planck and Boltzmann constants, respectively [45,46].

A mass loss (ML%) of 17.59 % as well as 9.96 % for Mn2O3/CQD1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2, respectively, appeared at 800 °C, as revealed in Fig. 6 . The degradation curves for Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 exhibited two distinct stages of decomposition. The first weight loss occurs between 27.14 and 242.78 and 43.86–231.84 °C due to the moisture content, with maximum temperatures of 88.43 and 153.03 °C and average ML of 11.12 and 5.58 % for Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2, respectively. The 2nd ML stage was between 251.19 and 344.60 and 231.84–305.13 °C, with average MLs of 6.47 and 4.38 % at temperatures of 294.03 and 248.23 °C for Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2, respectively, which decomposed the carbonized residues and was responsible for carbonization. According to previous findings, the ∑ ML for Mn2O3/CQD 2 (i.e., 17.59 %) is lower than that for Mn2O3/CQD 1 (i.e., 9.96 %), which indicates that Mn2O3/CQD 2 has greater thermal stability than Mn2O3/CQD1 [47]. An increase in ∑ Ea for Mn2O3/CQD 2 (53.86 kJ mol−1) compared to that for Mn2O3/CQD 1 (28.69 kJ mol−1) reveals a high degree of thermal stabilization of Mn2O3/CQD 2. Therefore, a high Ea is required for degradation.

The negative values for ∆S in both the Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 samples according to the study demonstrate that the framework is progressively decomposing and that degradation is not occurring spontaneously. The high ∆S value of −0.49 KJ mol−1 for Mn2O3/CQD 2 implies that its thermal decomposition was more complicated, requiring a greater investment of time and external energy for breakdown in comparison to Mn2O3/CQD 1 (−0.46 KJ mol−1).
Unlabelled Table

240.59

28.69
53.86

The contact angle measurements provided insights into the surface wettability of the Mn2O3/CQDs. The contact angles were determined to be 65.6 and 38.5 ° for Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 7 . A decrease in the contact angle suggested an increase in surface hydrophilicity. This change in wettability is attributed to the presence of Mn2O3, which may introduce functional groups or alter the surface energy of the material. The observed decrease in the contact angle is advantageous for supercapacitor applications. Enhanced surface hydrophilicity improves the wetting of the electrode material by the electrolyte, facilitating better ion penetration and charge transfer. This, in turn, can contribute to improved electrochemical performance and efficiency in supercapacitor devices.

The correlation between the contact angle and supercapacitor performance merits attention. A lower contact angle generally corresponds to improved accessibility of the electrolyte to the electrode material, which positively impacts the capacitance and charge-discharge kinetics. The results of contact angle measurements have practical implications for the design of supercapacitor devices. Mn2O3/CQD 2, with their altered surface wettability, presents an opportunity for the optimization of electrode materials to achieve superior electrochemical performance in supercapacitor applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of the as-prepared Mn2O3/CQD electrode
ce:section.ce:para: It is generally recognized that cyclic voltammetry provides a great deal of beneficial data on supercapacitive examination characteristics, current-potential response, and electrode-specific capacitance values. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to explore the supercapacitive characteristics of the Mn2O3/CQD electrodes in a system composed of three electrodes. The specific capacitance and the area under the CV curves are generally considered to represent a proportionate relationship. The Mn2O3/CQD 2 capacitance is significantly greater than that of Mn2O3/CQD 1, as shown in Fig. 8 .

This is explained by the fact that with a higher concentration of Mn2O3, there is likely to be a more active material available for electrochemical reactions. This can lead to a higher capacitance, as more charge can be stored at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Furthermore, a higher concentration of Mn2O3 may result in a larger surface area for the electrode material. Supercapacitors store charge at the electrode-electrolyte interface and a larger surface area allows for more charge storage. Higher concentrations might improve the overall conductivity of the electrode material, reducing the internal resistance and leading to better charge-discharge characteristics. Higher concentrations of Mn2O3 might result in better charge transport properties within the electrode material. This can lead to faster electron and ion transport, contributing to improved specific capacitance.

The CV curves of the Mn2O3/CQD composites at sweep rates ranging from 5 to 100 mV/s and a potential limit ranging from 0 to 0.8 V in a 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte are shown in Fig. 9 a and b. The corresponding nonlinear and analogous CV plots show the behavior of the working Mn2O3/CQD electrode as a pseudocapacitor. The quasi-rectangular forms of these curves suggest the optimal performance of electrical double-layer capacitance, which is characterized by quick charging and discharging. It has been suggested that the pseudocapacitance of Mn2O3/CQD is the consequence of a rapid redox reaction that is close to or on the electrode surface. This mechanism can be achieved by the intercalation of M+ ions. It is important to remember that sodium ions that have been separated from Na2SO4 can function as effective guest cations that can be readily intercalated and deintercalated through the structure of Mn2O3/CQD composites.

Interestingly, the Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrodes, as fabricated, display capacitances of 457.3 and 657 F g−1, respectively, at a sweep rate of 5 mV/s, demonstrating that they are capable of preserving electrochemical energy. The electrode's retention rate was demonstrated by capacitance measurements of 41 and 95.6 F g−1 for Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2, respectively, even at a high scan rate of 100 mV/s. The reversible CV pattern on the electrode remains consistent and well-defined. The electrochemical processes tend to slow when areas of the electrode surface are inaccessible at higher scan rates, which is the reason why the electrode capacitance decreases as the sweep rate increases. Nevertheless, the capacitance acquired at comparatively slow scan rates is believed to be the result of the surface of electroactive materials being entirely consumed [48]. The electrical charge storage efficiency of pseudocapacitive electrodes can be attributed to two factors. First, the diffusion process occurs when electrolyte ions are introduced into the material, triggering an electrochemical reaction that can be reversed at the electrode's active region. Second, the capacitive behavior results from the ability to store charges through the process of ion adsorption and desorption. The functional correlation between the current responsiveness and scanning rate was determined using CV curves, as illustrated in Fig. 9c. Pseudocapacitive electrodes store electrical charges in two distinct manners: first, by means of diffusion, which occurs when electrolyte ions penetrate into the material and trigger a reversible redox reaction on the electrode's active sites; and second, by means of capacitive behavior, which is caused by the charge storage capacity associated with ion adsorption/desorption to the electroactive surface [5]. In order to gain a better understanding of how each component contributes to the total capacitance of the Mn2O3/CQD electrode, CV curves were employed to figure out the functional relationship between scanning rate, and current response.
(6)
i
V
=
k 1
υ +
k 2
υ
1 / 2

The constants k1 and k2 can be determined by calculating the slope and intercept of the linear graph of i(V)/υ1/2 vs. υ1/2. The slope and y-axis intercept are intended to represent the capacitive and diffusive currents, respectively [5]. The plot of i(V)/υ1/2 vs. υ1/2, obtained from the Mn2O3/CQD electrodes, has been analyzed to distinguish between the redox and capacitive behaviors during the faradic process. The ratios of diffusion and capacitive behaviors of the Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrode as shown in Fig. 10 were calculated. The results indicate the separation of capacitive (k1υ) and diffusion (k2υ1/2) currents for the Mn2O3/CQD2 electrode at 5 and 100 mV/s. The contribution of the diffusion component to the Mn2O3/CQD2 electrode decreases as the scan rate increases. Specifically, it was observed to be 78 % at 5 mV/s and 18 % at 100 mV/s. This decrease is attributed to the fact that at higher scan rates, there is sufficient time for the diffusion and intercalation of electrolyte ions within the electrode channels.

For accurate measurements of the electrochemical supercapacitive responses of electroactive materials, the galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) approach is the proper approach. Chronopotentiometry (E vs. time), an output of the GCD, shows the potential response over time when an electrode is supplied with a constant current [49]. The charge/discharge profiles for Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 among a range of current densities (1–10 A g−1) are provided in Fig. 11a and b. Whenever Na+ ions and Mn2O3 are involved during electrochemical processes, nonlinear charge-discharge curves imply a quickly reversible redox pathway. The nontriangular GCD shapes appear to match the CV curves very well. The lower Coulombic efficiencies can be mainly attributed to the movement of soluble ions by means of the membrane, and insufficient insertion of the electrode during the discharge stage contributes to a decreasing capacitance at the full discharge stage. However, the shorter time frame for traversing soluble active species causes the Coulombic efficiency to gradually increase with increasing current density. At a current density of 1 A g−1, the calculated specific capacitances of the Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrodes are 426 and 612 F g−1, respectively.

A further essential factor when considering the potential utilization of electrodes is their rate capability. Fig. 11c shows an apparent reduction in capacity with increasing current density, which was triggered by the electrolyte's diminished ability to access a high surface area at high current densities. Furthermore, the electrode acquired a capacitive retention of 53.2 % for Mn2O3/CQD 2, revealing a large surface area as well as numerous active locations that are readily exposed to the ions of the electrolyte, which explains the favorable rate capability of the Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrode.

Furthermore, an investigation was carried out on the cycling characteristics of the developed Mn2O3/CQD electrodes at a high current density (10 A g−1). The cycle life, which is a highly measurable parameter of functional supercapacitors, is illustrated in Fig. 12 . After 5000 cycles, the electrode capacity still retained excellent capacitance. These findings demonstrate that the Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrode is superior to other reported Mn2O3/CQD 1 electrodes. After cycling, the surface wettability increases, making it easier for the electrolyte ions to continuously diffuse into the electrode microstructure with better routes that can reach deeper particles. This activation effect causes an increase in capacitance during electrochemical cycling as a result of diffusion enhancement. This may also be a result of the electrode material's superior architecture design, which incorporates carbon into Mn2O3/CQD to form conductive channels for electron and ion transport.

EIS has proven to be one of the most successful approaches for determining the external resistance as well as the charge transfer mechanism at the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte. The EIS data for the as-prepared Mn2O3/CQD 1 as well as Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrodes fitted with an equivalent circuit are provided in Fig. 13 . The equivalent circuit diagram (inset) that was employed to fit the EIS data comprises four components: the Warburg resistance Zw, the double layer capacitance CPE, the charge transfer resistance Rct, and the bulky solution resistance Rs. The Nyquist plots of the electrodes reveal a Warburg line or a straight line within the low-frequency region and an extremely small semicircle in the high-frequency region. The Nyquist curve at low frequencies is nearly vertical, which suggests that the electrochemistry of the samples is comparable to that of a perfect capacitor. In the same low-frequency range, a more inclined curve is visible for the Mn2O3/CQD electrode, indicating a predominance of pseudocapacitance. The Rct and Rs values are based on the described equivalent circuit diagram. It was determined that the material has a bulk resistance (Rs) of Mn2O3/CQD 1 and Mn2O3/CQD 2 around 91.3, 79.6 Ω. The equivalent series resistance RS and charge-transfer resistance Rct were smaller for the Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrode. Additionally, Mn2O3/CQD 2 has a greater Warburg resistance than Mn2O3/CQD 1, indicating a shorter diffusion channel for the transfer of ions. The capacitive behavior of the Mn2O3/CQD 2 nanocomposite was inferior to that of Mn2O3/CQD 1. The Mn2O3/CQD 2 samples exhibited maximum phase angles of 71° and 80°, according to the Bode plots (Fig. 13b). A perfect capacitor will exhibit a phase angle of exactly 90°. The EIS data verify the CV results and confirm that the Mn2O3/CQD 2 electrode is more effective than the Mn2O3/CQD 1 electrode.

According to Table 1 , we compared the as-prepared electrode to other electrodes in electrochemical systems. We realized it inspiring that our electrode, received superior capacitance performance in comparison to earlier studies.

In conclusion, Mn2O3-decorated carbon quantum dots derived from agricultural waste present a compelling avenue for the development of eco-friendly and high-performance supercapacitors. The synthesis process involves a green and cost-effective method, harnessing the inherent carbon-rich composition of agricultural waste. The environmentally friendly synthesis process aligns with the principles of green chemistry, emphasizing sustainability in materials design. Structural analyses, including XRD and TEM, confirmed the successful synthesis of Mn2O3/CQD2 with well-defined crystal structures and nanoscale morphologies. XPS and IR studies provide insights into the surface composition and chemical bonding, highlighting the synergistic interaction between Mn2O3 and carbon quantum dots. SEM-EDX mapping elucidates the spatial distribution of Mn2O3 within the carbon matrix. The electrochemical measurements revealed exceptional performance metrics, including a high specific capacitance and high energy density, indicating that Mn2O3/CQD is a promising candidate for high-performance supercapacitors. Cyclic voltammetry and charge-discharge studies demonstrate the outstanding high-rate capability of this material, underscoring its suitability for rapid energy storage and delivery of Mn2O3/CQD2 612 F g−1 at 1 A/g. Furthermore, the investigation of cycle stability underscores the robustness of Mn2O3/CQD, with minimal capacitance degradation observed over numerous cycles. This study not only advances the understanding of hybrid materials for supercapacitors but also emphasizes the significance of repurposing agricultural waste for sustainable energy storage technologies.

Sherief A. Al Kiey: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hebat-Allah S. Tohamy: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Research Centre for their technical support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manganese oxide(III)/carbon hybrids with interesting morphologies as improved active materials for supercapacitors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ engineering of urchin-like reduced graphene oxide–Mn2O3–Mn3O4 nanostructures for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.T. Chidembo, S.H. Aboutalebi, K. Konstantinov, C.J. Jafta, H.K. Liu, K.I. Ozoemena, In situ engineering of urchin-like reduced graphene oxide–Mn2O3–Mn3O4 nanostructures for supercapacitors, RSC Advances 4(2) (2014) 886-892.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A cost effective, highly porous, manganese oxide/carbon supercapacitor material with high rate capability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Wang, Y. Ma, H. Wang, J. Key, D. Brett, S. Ji, S. Yin, P.K. Shen, A cost effective, highly porous, manganese oxide/carbon supercapacitor material with high rate capability, Journal of Materials Chemistry A 4(15) (2016) 5390-5394.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mn2O3/carbon aerogel microbead composites synthesized by in situ coating method for supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, L. Liu, X. Wang, L. Yi, C. Hu, X. Zhang, Mn2O3/carbon aerogel microbead composites synthesized by in situ coating method for supercapacitors, Materials Science and Engineering: B 176(15) (2011) 1232-1238.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of a metal–organic framework-derived Mn 2 O 3 nanowire coated three-dimensional graphene network for high-performance free-standing supercapacitor electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Ji, H. Zhou, J. Zhang, Y. Dan, H. Yang, A. Yuan, Facile synthesis of a metal–organic framework-derived Mn 2 O 3 nanowire coated three-dimensional graphene network for high-performance free-standing supercapacitor electrodes, Journal of materials chemistry A 4(21) (2016) 8283-8290.
